overview: The Regex Filter processes any text-based format where translatable content can be captured using regular expressions. You define rules with regex patterns and associate capturing groups with roles (source, target, identifier, note) and actions (extract content, extract strings, comment, section start/end). Rules are evaluated in list order from top to bottom, with the filter searching for the first matching rule at each position in the document.

extraction.startString.help:
Characters that mark the beginning of a string literal in the document. Each character defines a separate way to start a string — entering `"'` means either `"` or `'` can begin a string.

If you define multiple start characters, you **must** define the same number of end characters in `endString`, with positions corresponding 1:1.
extraction.startString.values: One or more characters. Each character is a separate string delimiter. For example, `"` for double-quoted strings, or `"'` for both double and single-quoted strings.

dependsOn.condition: must have the same number of characters, with positions corresponding 1:1

extraction.endString.help:
Characters that mark the end of a string literal. If multiple beginning characters are defined, you must define an equal number of end characters, where each end character's position corresponds to its matching beginning character.

For example, if `startString` is `"'` then `endString` must also be two characters like `"'`.
extraction.endString.values: One or more characters, positionally matched to `startString`.

dependsOn.condition: must have the same number of characters, with positions corresponding 1:1

extraction.regexOptions.help:
Bitmask of Java `java.util.regex.Pattern` flags applied to **all** rules. Common flags:

- `2` — `CASE_INSENSITIVE` — ignore letter case differences
- `8` — `MULTILINE` — `^` and `$` match at line boundaries, not just start/end of input
- `32` — `DOTALL` — dot (`.`) also matches line-feed characters

Combine by adding values (e.g., `40` = DOTALL + MULTILINE). To override for a single rule, use the `(?idmsux-idmsux)` construct directly in that rule's pattern.
extraction.regexOptions.values: Integer bitmask of `java.util.regex.Pattern` flags. Common values: `0` (none), `2` (CASE_INSENSITIVE), `8` (MULTILINE), `32` (DOTALL), `40` (DOTALL + MULTILINE).

extraction.extractOuterStrings.help:
Extracts string literals found outside the scope of extraction rules. Strings are delimited by the `startString`/`endString` characters.

**Note:** The wiki indicates this option is not yet fully implemented.
notes: The option *Extract strings outside the rules* is listed as **not yet implemented** in the Limitations section.

extraction.oneLevelGroups.help:
Controls whether sections (groups) created by *Start a section* rules are nested or flat. When enabled, any previously opened section is automatically closed when a new one starts — you only need *Start a section* rules, no *End a section* rules.

When disabled, each *Start a section* action **must** have a corresponding *End a section* action.

extraction.mimeType.help:
The MIME type to associate with extracted content. This value identifies the document type and may affect how text is written back to the original format.

In most cases `text/plain` is appropriate.
extraction.mimeType.values: A valid MIME type string, e.g., `text/plain`, `text/html`.

output.localizeOutside.help:
Makes content **outside** the scope of localization directives translatable. This lets you mark up fewer parts of the source document — only the parts you want to *exclude* need directives.

Only effective when `useLd` is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for this option to have any effect

output.useBSlashEscape.help: Treats backslash-prefixed characters as escape sequences (e.g., `\"` is an escaped quote, not the end of a string). Used when parsing string boundaries defined by `startString`/`endString`.
examples: `\"` — escaped double quote inside a double-quoted string

dependsOn.condition: typically only one escape style should be active

output.removeBSlashEscape.help: Strips backslash escape prefixes from extracted content. When enabled, `\"` becomes `"` in the extracted text.

output.useDoubleCharEscape.help: Treats doubled characters as escape sequences (e.g., `""` is an escaped quote inside a double-quoted string).
examples: `""` — escaped double quote when strings are delimited by `"`

dependsOn.condition: typically only one escape style should be active

output.useLd.help:
Enables recognition of **localization directives** in the input file. When disabled, any localization directives present in the file are ignored.

Localization directives allow fine-grained control over which parts of the document are extracted for translation.

subfilters.subFilter.help: Specifies a sub-filter to apply to extracted content. The extracted text is passed through the named filter for further processing, enabling nested format handling (e.g., HTML content embedded in a text-based format).
subfilters.subFilter.values: A filter configuration ID (e.g., `okf_html`), or empty string for no sub-filter.

limitations: The **entire document is loaded into memory** to apply regular expressions — this may cause issues with very large files.
The option *Extract strings outside the rules* is **not yet implemented**.
processingNotes: **Encoding detection**: Uses Unicode BOM if present; otherwise falls back to the default encoding specified when opening the document.
**Output encoding**: The filter cannot detect or update encoding declarations in the document. BOM is preserved in UTF-8 output only if the input was also UTF-8 and had a BOM.
**Line breaks**: Output preserves the same line-break style as the original input.
**Rule evaluation order**: Rules are evaluated in list order. At each position, the filter uses the first rule that matches, applies its action, then advances past the match.
**Capturing groups** are numbered by counting opening parentheses left to right. Group 0 is the whole match. In `(A)(B(C))`: group 1 = `(A)`, group 2 = `(B(C))`, group 3 = `(C)`.

examples.title: Simple key=value extraction
examples.description: Extract translatable text from a file where each line is `[ID]=Text` or `[ID]:Text`. The regex uses three capturing groups: group 1 for the identifier, group 2 for the separator, and group 3 for the source text.
examples.config:
```
Rule regex: ^\[(.*?)\](=|:)(.*?)$
Action: Extract the content of the source group
Source group: 3
Identifier group: 1
```
examples.output:
```xml
<trans-unit id="1" resname="ID1" xml:space="preserve">
  <source xml:lang="en">Text for ID1</source>
</trans-unit>
<trans-unit id="2" resname="ID2" xml:space="preserve">
  <source xml:lang="en">Text for ID2</source>
</trans-unit>
```

examples.title: SRT subtitle extraction
examples.description: The built-in `okf_regex-srt` configuration handles SubRip Text subtitle files, extracting subtitle text while preserving timing codes as non-translatable structure.

examples.title: Metadata rules (FPRM only)
examples.description: Metadata rules add matched regex content as metadata to TextUnits. These are only configurable via the `.fprm` parameter file, not through the UI.
